transcript.speaker: Vinod
transcript.text: Good afternoon. Final weekly call for Sprint 4. I want a complete picture before we head into the weekend. UAT starts Monday morning. Give me the full status. Rupali, go ahead.

transcript.speaker: Rupali
transcript.text: Sprint 4 Week 2 headline — we're in a significantly stronger position than we were last Friday. The async retry queue is built and deployed to prod. Redis is running clean. All 34 test cases are now passing on prod. Client has been briefed and UAT is confirmed for Monday. I'll let the team give you the specifics.

transcript.speaker: Vinod
transcript.text: Ajay, the 34 of 34 — confirm that directly for me.

transcript.speaker: Ajay
transcript.text: Confirmed sir. I ran the full suite on prod this morning. All 34 passing. I want to specifically call out the 2 intermittent failures that were open since last Friday — the ones we were seeing roughly 3 out of 5 runs on prod. Both of those are now passing consistently. I ran each of them 5 times this morning. 5 out of 5 both times. The async queue resolved them completely.

transcript.speaker: Rakesh
transcript.text: The async queue being done is the single biggest win of this week. Varsha, walk us through what was actually delivered.

transcript.speaker: Varsha
transcript.text: Async queue handles three scenarios — payment failure with automatic retry, gateway-down fallback with transaction persistence to DB, and an idempotency key check to prevent duplicate transactions if the same order is submitted twice. Redis was provisioned at 2:15pm Wednesday after Vinod sir sent the escalation email to infra. I switched from mock to real infra immediately, had the consumer logic, retry scheduler, and dead-letter queue handling done by 9pm, and deployed to prod at 10:30pm. I ran manual end-to-end tests across all three flows Thursday morning — all working correctly.

transcript.speaker: Vivek
transcript.text: Redis has been running clean for over 40 hours now. I set up a monitoring alert — if the pending queue depth crosses 100 items, I get an immediate notification. Nothing has fired. The infrastructure is solid.

transcript.speaker: Rupali
transcript.text: I also want to acknowledge the prodroduction reliability improvement this week. We had 2 new intermittent failures in prod on Tuesday evening — Varsha confirmed them in Thursday's standup. Those are now resolved with the async queue. So prod is cleaner going into UAT than it was at Phase 1 go-live.

transcript.speaker: Suraj
transcript.text: On frontend — Thursday's session with Varsha and Ajay was very productive. We went through the business edge cases Ajay had been thinking about — browser back button during payment, slow connection spinner, the double-submission scenario. I fixed 2 things that weren't handled: back button during payment now shows a warning modal instead of breaking the flow, and the loading spinner on slow connections was missing. Both tested on iOS and Android this morning. I also sent client a screen-by-screen UI walkthrough document so their testers know what to expect on Monday.

transcript.speaker: Vinod
transcript.text: Client received the walkthrough doc?

transcript.speaker: Rupali
transcript.text: Yes sir. Confirmed receipt at 11am today. Their lead tester replied and said it was very helpful. Ajay also sent the full UAT scenario document — 34 scenarios with expected outcomes and edge case descriptions. They have everything they need.

transcript.speaker: Ajay
transcript.text: I want to mention the double-payment scenario specifically because it was a real gap. If a user hit pay twice — whether by accident or browser issue — we had no protection. Varsha added an idempotency key check last night. I tested it — second request on the same order returns a clear message, no duplicate transaction. That would have been an embarrassing UAT failure on Monday without it.

transcript.speaker: Vinod
transcript.text: Good. Monday UAT — what are the logistics?

transcript.speaker: Rupali
transcript.text: UAT starts at 10am Monday. Client's full payments team — four testers plus their tech lead. From our side, Rakesh and Ajay are on standby throughout the day. Varsha is available for any backend queries. I'll be in the session to coordinate. We agreed in this morning's standup — if anything comes up, we take it to the internal group first before responding to client. No individual panic responses on client calls.

transcript.speaker: Rakesh
transcript.text: That protocol is important. Whatever comes up Monday — no matter how small — we align internally first, then respond together. Client needs to see a calm, coordinated team.

transcript.speaker: Varsha
transcript.text: Agreed. And browser close during an active payment is also handled now — transaction completes in the background via the queue and email confirmation is sent. When the user comes back and reloads, the page reconciles to the correct state from the server. So even that edge case is covered.

transcript.speaker: Vinod
transcript.text: And the Sprint 5 scope — where does that stand?

transcript.speaker: Rupali
transcript.text: Wednesday's scope call with client settled it. Sprint 5 is being split into two phases. Phase A covers inventory sync and returns flow — three weeks. Phase B is admin dashboard — three additional weeks. Client also mandated that the async retry queue — which we just completed — was to be treated as item zero before any Sprint 5 work. That's done now. The technical risk document Rakesh and Varsha put together on Tuesday was very effective — client couldn't dispute the numbers and it gave them confidence we understood the risks clearly.

transcript.speaker: Rakesh
transcript.text: The returns flow dependency on the async queue is the key point for Sprint 5. Refund reversals need the fallback mechanism — if the gateway is down during a refund and we have no queue, the customer doesn't get their money back. That would have been a serious problem. We've solved it before Sprint 5 starts, which is the right order.

transcript.speaker: Vivek
transcript.text: One more thing on Sprint 5 — Rupali confirmed at Monday's standup that I'm in Sprint 5 planning from day one. I'm also following up on the prod-parity staging request Ajay and I raised in Wednesday's scope call. Vinod sir sent the infra email. Haven't heard back yet but I'm expecting a response early next week.

transcript.speaker: Vinod
transcript.text: I'll chase infra Monday if no response by then. Environment parity is not optional for Sprint 5. We are not having another SSL cert discovery on Day 4. Okay — closing thoughts before we wrap up.

transcript.speaker: Rakesh
transcript.text: Sprint 4 was hard. I said in Monday's standup that we lost almost 2 full days to environment issues that were outside the development team's control — Day 1 staging config, Day 4 SSL cert. But the team absorbed those losses and kept delivering. Going into UAT with all 34 tests passing and a properly built async queue is a strong outcome given how Monday of Week 1 looked.

transcript.speaker: Vinod
transcript.text: Well said. I want to acknowledge specifically — Varsha and Ajay were working late multiple nights during this sprint. That kind of effort is what got us here. Rupali, make sure the retrospective is scheduled. Lessons learned from this sprint need to be documented formally so Sprint 5 starts differently.

transcript.speaker: Rupali
transcript.text: Retrospective is scheduled today — 5pm, same team. I'll send the agenda so everyone can bring their notes. Alright, that's the call. Clean UAT on Monday. Retro this evening. Good work this sprint, everyone.
